coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of Pangium edule kernel biomass-derived NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film for visible light-assisted methylene blue reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Waluyo, Rachmat
Hadju, Aljufri
Rati, Yolanda
Marlina, Resti
Darma, Yudi
coredata.dc:description: Water pollution from dye waste presents a pressing environmental challenge that urgently requires innovative and sustainable solutions. In this regard, reusable visible-light-driven photocatalysts prepared using renewable resources are essential to overcome the problem. This research aims to develop Pangium edule (PE) kernel biomass-derived NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite films for reusable visible light-assisted photodegradation. The effect of incorporating NCQDs and TiO2 into the PVA matrix was investigated for its photodegradation performance using methylene blue (MB) as a model pollutant dye. The characterization results indicated cooperative interaction between NCQDs and TiO2 in terms of Ti–O–C bonds. Moreover, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA promotes an increase in the photodegradation performance of MB, which depends on the NCQDs’ content. Interestingly, beyond a certain threshold, augmenting the number of NCQDs exhibited a diminishing effect on photodegradation performance. The NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 nanocomposite film demonstrated optimal catalytic efficiency of 98.2 % and exhibited stability as a photocatalyst for MB degradation over five cycles. Overall, utilizing an easy synthetic route for NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film demonstrate outstanding photodegradation performance, offering an eco-friendly and sustainable water remediation technology.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution from dye waste presents a pressing environmental challenge that urgently requires innovative and sustainable solutions. In this regard, reusable visible-light-driven photocatalysts prepared using renewable resources are essential to overcome the problem. This research aims to develop Pangium edule (PE) kernel biomass-derived NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite films for reusable visible light-assisted photodegradation. The effect of incorporating NCQDs and TiO2 into the PVA matrix was investigated for its photodegradation performance using methylene blue (MB) as a model pollutant dye. The characterization results indicated cooperative interaction between NCQDs and TiO2 in terms of Ti–O–C bonds. Moreover, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA promotes an increase in the photodegradation performance of MB, which depends on the NCQDs’ content. Interestingly, beyond a certain threshold, augmenting the number of NCQDs exhibited a diminishing effect on photodegradation performance. The NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 nanocomposite film demonstrated optimal catalytic efficiency of 98.2 % and exhibited stability as a photocatalyst for MB degradation over five cycles. Overall, utilizing an easy synthetic route for NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film demonstrate outstanding photodegradation performance, offering an eco-friendly and sustainable water remediation technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution is a significant global challenge attributed to inadequate wastewater treatment practices within the textile industry. Various dyes are used in the textile industries during production and most of them are discarded as waste. The used dyes contain carcinogenic and mutagenic compounds. So, it can lead to environmental pollution if the wastes are released directly or treated improperly as well as can be harmful for living organisms and the environment [1]. Various physical, chemical, and biological treatment techniques such as coagulation, flocculation, reverse osmosis, filtration, adsorption, photocatalysis, microbiology, and ozonation had been carried out for water remediation [2–4]]. Among these methods, photocatalysis is a widely used advanced oxidation process (AOP) water treatment process due to its efficiency, low operating cost, effective decomposition of organic waste, and environmentally friendly [5–8]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been considered as a potential material to degrade several types of water pollutants due to its good photocatalytic activity, low cost, nontoxicity, strong oxidizing capability, and chemical inertness [9–11]. However, the application of TiO2 as a photocatalyst has faced challenges due to its restricted absorption spectrum in UV light and the rapid recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers [12–14]. The fact that UV light constitutes only approximately 4 % of the solar spectrum reaching the Earth's surface is widely recognized [15]. Therefore, one strategy to enhance the photodegradation performance of TiO2 under visible light is by combining with other materials in order to construct hybrid composite materials.

Recent studies have considered carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for promising photocatalysts in coupling with TiO2 [16]. CQDs are a new type of fluorescence nanomaterial with many advantages, such as good biocompatibility, water dispersibility, photostability, chemical inertness, excellent optical properties, easy synthesis procedures, and low cost [17,18]. Moreover, CQDs have been synthesized from various natural resources of biomass, including banana peels [19], orange peels [20], cassava peels [19], and grape peels [21]. The selection of these materials is based on the carbon element's content.

Pangium edule (PE) is a tropical plant that grows indigenously in Southeast Asia and Papua, including Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, and Papua New Guinea [22]. Cultivated extensively in Indonesia, PE or locally known as kepayang, has become a primary ingredient in traditional Indonesian cuisine, particularly in the form of a rich dark meat soup known as rawon. In the utilization of PE fruit for culinary purposes, only 30 % of its weight is utilized, while the remaining 70 % consists of kernels, which are lignocellulosic biomass, that has not been used optimally in nature [23,24]. Currently, several studies related to the utilization of PE kernel waste as biopellet fuel have been carried out and have given good results and meet biopellet standards [25,26]. The composition of PE kernel includes cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin with a fixed carbon value of 92.15 % [25]. Therefore, the PE kernel can be an excellent candidate to be a carbon precursor in the synthesis of CQDs.

Despite the attractive nature of CQDs, certain researchers have reported that pristine CQDs demonstrate inadequate photosensitization and a restricted light absorption range. For these issues, researchers have explored the method of nitrogen doping using urea on CQDs. This approach aims to modify the electronic structure of CQDs, resulting in enhanced broad light absorption and improved photoluminescence properties [27–29]. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) would be integrated into heterostructures and play various roles in heterogeneous photocatalysis. In this case, NCQDs can act as electron mediators, visible light harvesters, photosensitizers, and reduce charge recombination [30,31]. Although many works on modifying NCQDs based nanocomposite have been carried out, most of them require complex, lengthy synthesis procedures, using harsh reagents or conditions, tedious post-synthesis procedures, and lack the practicality of reuse [32,33]. Therefore, there is a great urgency to explore an easier method for the mass production of NCQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalysts.

Currently, powder-based photocatalysts required further separation procedures from dye reaction medium because the reaction itself. For this purpose, NCQDs/TiO2 was incorporated into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix via an easy synthesis route, allowing an easy recovery and recycling of the catalytic system with high stability. PVA is polymer that has terrific properties such as cheap, solubility, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, high transparency, and easiness in handling [34]. Hence, the PE kernel biomass-derived NCQDs/TiO2/PVA is promising photocatalyst for photodegradation applications.

In this study, a facile and efficient solution-casting method was employed to synthesize a nanocomposite film of NCQDs/TiO2/PVA using Pangium edule kernel biomass as carbon source. The concentration of NCQDs in the nanocomposite film was systematically varied to ascertain the optimal photodegradation performance. Subsequently, the chosen optimal sample was subjected to a reusability assessment over five cycles, demonstrating consistent and stable photodegradation performance. Eventually, a possible mechanism for enhanced visible light utilization in the NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film was proposed.

PE kernel was locally purchased from local market in Dago, Bandung, Indonesia. Urea (CAS 57-13-6) was obtained from Pudak Scientific, Indonesia. Methylene blue (MB) was purchased from Rofa Laboratorium Center, Indonesia. PVA (CAS 9002-89-5). TiO2 (anatase powder, 99.8 % trace metals basis, CAS 1317-70-0) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. No additional purification was performed on the chemicals and distilled water was utilized for the whole experiment.

PE kernels were washed using distilled water to remove soluble impurities and dried thoroughly. The dried PE kernels were mashed into powder using a mortar. Then, it was filtered using 40 mesh sieves. A total of 1 g of powdered PE kernel and 2 g of urea were dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water. After stirring vigorously for 10 min, the mixture was heated on a hot plate for 2 h at 350 °C, forming a solid black powder. To ensure consistent temperatures throughout the synthesis process, thermocouples were placed on a hot plate. The powder was dissolved again into 20 mL using sonication for 30 min. The solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper (Grade 1) and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min. The synthesized NCQDs were stored in the dark at ambient temperature for further use.

Nanocomposite films were produced by the solution casting method and set to a total volume of 15 mL. Briefly, 10 wt% PVA solution was prepared by dissolving into distilled water at 90 °C for 30 min. The PVA solution was continuously mixed at room temperature for 30 min to produce a homogenized PVA solution. The TiO2/PVA nanocomposite was prepared by mixing 0.1 g of TiO2 powder into PVA solution. The NCQDs/PVA nanocomposite was prepared by mixing 2 mL NCQDs into PVA solution. The NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposites were prepared at different volumes of NCQDs (1, 2, and 3 mL). The samples are denoted to be NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-1, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2, and NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-3, respectively. Notably, for NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposites, TiO2 powder was sonicated with NCQDs solution for 30 min. Then, it was added PVA solution and stirred at 250 rpm at room temperature for 2 h. The homogeneous gel solution obtained was poured into a petri dish and heated at 50 °C for 2 h. The thickness of the nanocomposite films was controlled using 0.15 g in 3 × 3 cm square shape.

The performance of nanocomposite films as photocatalysts was evaluated using MB solution (100 mL, 10 ppm, pH 6) in a 4 cm diameter of beaker glass. Prior to irradiation, the nanocomposite film was mixed with MB solution and magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The distance between the reactor and the xenon lamp in the PEC-L01 solar simulator (1000 W/m2) was fixed at 15 cm for 180 min. Then 3 mL of the sample was withdrawn every 30 min interval. Finally, the gathered samples were analyzed to UV–Vis (nanoCalc 2000) at 664 nm for the determination of MB dye concentration. The degradation efficiency (

The recycling test was performed to investigate the stability and reusability of the nanocomposite film for degradation MB under visible light irradiation through the same reaction conditions. After each cycle, the used sample was collected and then washed with distilled water and dried before being reused.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was taken with the HT7700 at an acceleration voltage of 120 kV. UV–vis spectrophotometer Agilent 8453 was used to obtain the NCQDs absorption spectrum. Photoluminescence (PL) of the NCQDs was recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-2700). Surface morphology was characterized using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) with the Thermo Scientific Quattro-S. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping was employed to examine the element distribution. XRD patterns were investigated using XRD Shimadzu MaximaX-7000 (Cu K
α
) with scanning range from 5
°

°
. FTIR patterns were obtained using UATR-FTIR instrument (Spectrum Two, Perkin Elmer, USA). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) of nanocomposite films were obtained using the UV–Vis U–3900H. PL spectra of nanocomposite films were measured using the Edinburgh F-7100 PL spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural, and optical properties of NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1 a shows the morphology of NCQDs, wherein they were quasi-spherical. The NCQDs exhibited an average diameter of (5.6 ± 1.5) nm (Fig. 1b). XRD analysis revealed carbon structure through an intense peak that confirms the graphitic nature of the NCQDs (Fig. 1c) at 24.2
°
and weak peak at 41.3
°
, and 54.6
°
2 θ
, corresponding to the (002), (100), and (004) plane, respectively [35–37]. The broadening peak of the XRD pattern was also associated with the small size of NCQDs. Additionally, the XRD pattern of the NCQDs revealed extra crystalline peaks at 22.3
°
, 27.4
°
, 28.9
°
, and 37.9
°
2 θ
, corresponding to the (112), (221), (003), and (330) crystal planes of urea [38]. Meanwhile, non-labelled peaks indicate miscellaneous organic by-products, showing the characteristics of biomass carbon-based materials produced from the heating process [39,40].

Fig. 1d depicts the FTIR spectrum, illustrating the presence of diverse functional groups on the surface of NCQDs. The pronounced and broad absorption peak observed between 3000 and 3700 cm−1 indicated the characteristic stretching vibrations of O–H bonds (3100-3700 cm−1) and N–H bonds (3300-3500 cm−1) [41]. The presence of hydroxyl and amine functional groups indicates hydrophilic, easily soluble in water, and conducive migration of charge carriers in order to intense photocatalytic activity [41,42]. Meanwhile, the absorption peaks at 2812 cm−1, 1664 cm−1, 1630 cm−1, 1465 cm−1, 1332 cm−1, 1168 cm−1, 1060 cm−1, and 782 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–H, CO, CN, CC, C–N, C–O, C–O–C, and C–OH stretching vibrations, respectively [43–45]. Moreover, the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups confirms the successful doping of N atoms. The surface oxygenated functional groups serve to impede the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Overall, the abundant surface functional groups of the NCQDs can function as a series of surface emissive sites, responsible for the optical properties exhibited by NCQDs.

Fig. 1e displays the UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of NCQDs, revealing characteristic absorption peaks at 223 nm and 278 nm with a tail across the visible region are assigned to CC and CO, respectively [46]. Fig. 1f shows the excitation-dependent photoluminescence behaviour of NCQDs. The peak intensity decreases through increased excitation wavelengths and shifts towards redshift. Furthermore, FTIR analysis supports this result, which shows NCQDs have abundant surface sites that make of distinct emissive traps on the surface [47]. The maximum emission of NCQDs was observed at 445 nm for an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. This result agrees with the physical appearance of NCQDs solution under UV light exposure, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1e.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural and optical properties analysis of nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 a illustrates the morphology of TiO2, wherein TiO2 was quasi-spherical. The inset Fig. 2a shows the TiO2 samples have average diameter of (133.4
±
42.2) nm. The NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 had been characterized with FE-SEM to investigate the morphology of the prepared photocatalyst. Fig. 2b represents the surface of NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2, wherein the homogenous dispersion of NCQDs and TiO2 in the PVA matrix. The elemental composition of NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 based on Fig. 2c reveals the presence of C, N, O, and Ti elements. Furthermore, the elemental mappings (Fig. 2d–h) illustrate the distribution of Ti, O, C, and N elements, confirming the successful synthesis of NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film.

The chemical structures of nanocomposite films were characterized using XRD and FTIR tests. Fig. 3 a shows the XRD spectra of pristine TiO2, pure PVA, NCQDs/PVA, TiO2/PVA, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite films. The peaks at 25.31°, 37.04°, 37.39°, 38.67°, 48.17°, 54.03° and 55.21°
2 θ
are assigned to (101), (103), (004), (112), (200), (105) and (211) crystal planes of anatase phase TiO2 (COD 9015929), which indicate the TiO2 samples have tetragonal structure with space group I41/amd, a = 3.78
Å
, b = 3.78
Å
, and c = 9.51
Å
. Meanwhile, the pure PVA film has two peaks at 19.8° and 40.5°
2 θ
, which corresponds to (101) and (102) planes, indicating the semicrystalline properties [48]. The peaks at 25.3°, 28.9°, and 41.3°
2 θ
correspond to (002), (003), and (100) planes, respectively. It confirms the existence of graphitic structure in NCQDs/PVA and NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposites. In addition, the peaks at 25.3°, 36.9°, 37.8°, 38.6°, 48.1°, 53.9°, and 55.1° correspond to (101), (103), (004), (112), (200), (105), and (211), respectively, confirming the existence of TiO2 in TiO2/PVA and NCQDs/TiO2/PVA. The diffraction peak of NCQDs at 28.9° 2θ disappeared in the NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-1 nanocomposite film. For the NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 and NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-3 nanocomposite films, the diffraction peak of NCQDs at 2θ of 28.9° appeared and increased with increasing NCQDs content. In addition, the XRD spectra of nanocomposite films have a similar crystalline characteristic peak with a pure PVA film diffraction peak at 19.8° 2
θ
. It indicates TiO2 and NCQDs are not influence the crystal structure of PVA. However, the intensity of this peak decreased with the introduction of TiO2 and NCQDs in the PVA matrix, which indicates reduction of PVA crystal structure with the incorporation of TiO2 and NCQDs [49].

Fig. 3b shows the FTIR spectra of pristine TiO2, pure PVA, TiO2/PVA, NCQDs/PVA, and NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite films. The peak area at ∼3300 cm−1 and 1432 cm−1 in pure PVA and TiO2/PVA is associated with O–H and CH2, respectively. In addition, the peak area at ∼3300 cm−1 in NCQD-containing nanocomposites is also associated with N–H groups. Meanwhile, the absorption peaks observed in all nanocomposites at 2925 cm−1, 1670 cm−1, 1081 cm−1, and 838 cm−1 were attributed to C–H, CO, C–O, and C–OH stretching vibrations, respectively. Notably, with the increase in NCQDs content, the intensity of the CO vibration peak became more pronounced. Whereas, the presence of OH groups (∼3300 cm−1) in nanocomposite films decreased after incorporating NCQDs. This phenomenon is presumably due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between PVA and NCQDs. The slight changes in the films containing NCQDs clearly indicate the possibility of NCQDs attaching to the OH groups in the side chains of PVA, leading to increased interchain conjugation of PVA [50,51]. Furthermore, the stretching band of C–O in the carboxyl group was related to its crystallinity [52]. Therefore, the semicrystalline nature of the PVA was confirmed, as also confirmed in its XRD spectrum. In addition, it proved the crystallinity of the PVA had also decreased with the incorporation of TiO2 and NCQDs in the PVA matrix, as confirmed by XRD spectra. The characteristics absorption peaks such as CC stretching vibrations at 1438 cm−1 and C–N stretching vibrations at 1360 cm−1, shown in all NCQD-containing nanocomposites, further confirm the successful combination of NCQDs in the nanocomposite [41,53]. The absorption region below 800 cm−1 of pristine TiO2 and TiO2/PVA can be assigned to the Ti–O–Ti stretching vibration [54,55]. Compared with TiO2/PVA, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite films are redshift and narrower of absorption below 800 cm−1 due to a combination of Ti–O–Ti and Ti–O–C stretching vibrations [56]. The formation of Ti–O–C bonds implies a conducive interfacial charge transfer, thereby playing an essential role in the photodegradation performance.

The absorption properties of nanocomposite films were investigated by UV–Vis DRS, as shown in Fig. 3c. The TiO2/PVA has minimal absorption within the visible spectrum, absorbing only light with wavelength shorter than 384 nm. Meanwhile, NCQDs/PVA has light absorption within the visible spectrum, showing its potential in increasing the availability of visible light absorption. The addition of NCQDs’ content on the nanocomposite film also extend the absorption in the visible light. As a result, the band gap energy of nanocomposite film decreases with the increasing NCQDs' content, which is caused by the formation of Ti–O–C bonds [57]. The reduced band gap energy is expected to enhance the sunlight-harvesting ability, generating more charge carriers, which can enhance the photodegradation performance. From Fig. 3d, it can be seen that the calculated band gap energy for TiO2/PVA, NCQDs/PVA, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-1, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2, and NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-3 are 3.23, 3.04, 3.20, 3.19, and 3.10 eV, respectively. These results are consistent with several literature reports which mention that the addition of NCQDs to TiO2 can reduce the band gap energy [16,41,58].

PL study was carried out to further investigate separation efficiency of charge carriers of photocatalysts. Fig. 3e shows the PL spectra of TiO2/PVA and NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 nanocomposite films. Interestingly, the PL intensity of NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 is lower than TiO2/PVA, which represents a lower recombination of electron-hole pairs. The lower recombination of electron-hole pairs is facilitated by the presence of Ti–O–C bonds, which further leads to enhanced photodegradation performance [57,59].

The photodegradation performance of nanocomposite films under visible light irradiation was evaluated using MB as the model pollutant. In addition, the pH of the environment has an impact on MB degradation. Therefore, the initial pH used in this study was controlled at pH 6. Before the photocatalytic reaction, the aqueous suspension was stirred for 30 min in a dark condition to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Fig. 4 a presents the photodegradation of MB (10 ppm, 100 mL) by nanocomposite films under visible light irradiation. The investigation of MB sample photodegradation was carried out first to determine the self-removal effect of MB towards light irradiation. It is found that the self-removal MB contributes to only 14 % of the photodegradation performance of nanocomposites. After 180 min of irradiation, nanocomposite films of TiO2/PVA, NCQDs/PVA, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-1, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2, and NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-3 can degrade MB (10 ppm) 71.1, 67.4, 90.6, 98.2, and 82.4 %, respectively. Furthermore, the degradation rate constants of nanocomposite films were calculated to gain insights into their photodegradation activity.

The degradation rate constants are demonstrated in Fig. 4b. The kinetic parameters of the reaction are given in the inset of Fig. 4b. These results indicate that both TiO2/PVA and NCQDs/PVA have low photodegradation performance. It is clear that the degradation of the MB dye was greatly enhanced by incorporating NCQDs and TiO2 on the PVA matrix, causing the photodegradation performance to increase seven times better than its self-degradation. Significantly, the highest degradation and rate constant were achieved by using NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2. The improved photodegradation performance by increasing the NCQDs’ content is attributed to the augmented availability of active sites. This increased availability facilitates more interactions between the free radicals generated during photocatalysis and MB molecules, leading to improved photodegradation efficiency [60]. Notably, the addition NCQDs enhanced the sunlight-harvesting capacity and interfacial charge separation of the system, as confirmed by UV–Vis DRS and PL analysis above. On the other hand, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-3 exhibited less degradation than nanocomposites with less NCQDs content. This condition might be caused by the optimum number of catalysts which have been achieved. An excessive amount of catalyst can lead to the agglomeration of nanoparticles, which act as recombination centers for photo-induced charge carriers. As a consequence, this reduces the number of available effective active sites, inhibiting the photocatalysis process and diminishing the overall photodegradation performance [41,61]. These observations are consistent with the results of Eskalen et al. [58], which demonstrated that the excess NCQDs content would increase the passive layers that lowered the photo adsorption efficiency of the system. It is essential to find an optimal catalyst dosage that maximizes the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction without causing agglomeration and recombination of charge carriers.

Additionally, the NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 with the highest photodegradation performance was tested using various initial concentrations of MB dye. Fig. 4c shows that the system's performance decreased at higher initial MB concentrations. After 180 min of irradiation, the system can degrade 10, 20, and 50 ppm of MB dye 98.2, 69.4, and 11.2 %, respectively. Decreased photodegradation of MB dye at high MB concentrations was due to the constant surface area of the catalyst, thereby reducing the surface-active sites [62,63]. Furthermore, an examination of the reusability performance of NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 was also performed to degrade MB (10 ppm, 100 mL) for five cycles. As we can see from Fig. 3d, the photodegradation performance in each cycle of degraded MB dye showed no significant changes in up to five cycles. This suggests that the photocatalyst has good reusability, good stability, and feasible for practical application in water remediation. Table 1 compares the results of this work with the photodegradation performance of other nanocomposite photocatalysts.

The remarkable photodegradation performance observed in the NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film can be primarily attributed to the synergistic interaction between NCQDs and TiO2. The incorporation of NCQDs into the nanocomposite resulted in an improved light-harvesting capacity within the visible light region, as confirmed by UV–vis DRS analysis. Furthermore, the efficient transfer of electrons across the heterojunction interfaces in the nanocomposite was facilitated by the presence of Ti–O–C bonds, leading to prolonged electron lifetime, as confirmed by PL analysis. Additionally, the presence of NCQDs exerted a photosensitizing effect, further enhancing the photocatalytic activity by generating a higher number of electrons [16,41]. Fig. 5 illustrates the potential mechanism underlying the enhanced photodegradation performance observed in the NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film. Upon visible light irradiation, the electrons in the valence band (VB) of TiO2 will be excited into conduction band (CB) left the holes in the VB. Furthermore, the photogenerated electrons can effectively migrate to the NCQDs, thus inhibiting charge recombination within the system [57,64]. As a result, oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions exist at the surface of the nanocomposite film because electrons and holes interact with O2 and H2O to form superoxide anions (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), respectively. These superoxide and hydroxyl radicals play a key role in degrading MB molecules, generating harmless byproducts like carbon dioxide and water [65,66]. In addition, the existence of amino groups on NCQDs can enhance the selectivity towards MB cationic dye by interacting with the dye molecules [67]. Based on these results, NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film can potentially be an excellent photocatalyst in the degradation of MB dye.

PE kernel biomass-derived NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite films were successfully prepared via a facile solution casting method. Incorporating NCQDs and TiO2 into the PVA matrix had significantly improved photodegradation performance through the synergistic effect of NCQDs, which promoted the utilization of visible light and inhibited electron-hole pairs recombination in the system. Furthermore, the optimal NCQDs/TiO2/PVA-2 exhibits stable photodegradation performance with a degradation efficiency of 98.2 % and retention of 96.0 % after being reused for five cycles. Overall, PE kernel biomass-derived NCQDs/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite film has great potential for reusable photocatalyst under visible light irradiation to support clean energy systems.

Rachmat Waluyo: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Aljufri Hadju: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yolanda Rati: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Resti Marlina: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yudi Darma: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
